did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (CNNs, Vision Transformers, and hybrid CNN-Transformer architectures) to perform automated skin lesion classification and melanoma detection using the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a comparative analysis of eight deep learning models (CNNs, ViTs, and hybrids) for melanoma detection using the ISIC dataset. It includes specific sections on Materials and Methods (dataset, preprocessing, model architectures, training parameters), Experimental Results (accuracy, AUC, efficiency metrics), and a Conclusion, indicating it is an original study rather than a review.

title: Comparative Analysis of Single and Hybrid CNN-Transformer Models for Melanoma Detection Using the ISIC Dataset
journal: Proceedings of CONF-SEML 2025 Symposium: Machine Learning Theory and Applications
brief_summary: This study conducted a comparative analysis of eight deep learning architectures, including traditional CNNs, residual networks, vision transformers, and hybrid models, for automated skin lesion classification and melanoma detection. The models were evaluated using the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, which comprised 3,267 dermatoscopic images divided into training and validation sets. Performance was assessed based on accuracy, AUC, and computational efficiency metrics such as inference time and memory usage. The ResNet+ViT hybrid architecture achieved the highest classification performance with an accuracy of 85.1% and an AUC of 0.932, outperforming all individual model types. However, these hybrid models incurred significantly higher computational costs compared to efficient models like MobileNetV2, which offered the best efficiency with 79.1% accuracy. The findings suggest that while hybrid CNN-Transformer models provide robust representations approaching dermatologist-level accuracy, trade-offs between performance and computational resources must be considered for practical deployment.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on melanoma detection as a binary classification task. The dataset contains 1,197 malignant cases in the training set and 300 in the validation set. Melanoma is described as the most aggressive and potentially lethal variant of skin cancer.

The study performs binary classification distinguishing between benign and malignant lesions. The dataset contains 1,440 benign cases in the training set and 330 in the validation set. The term 'benign' is used as the counterpart to 'malignant' (melanoma) in the classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign and malignant (melanoma) categories
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions using the ISIC dataset. The models predict discrete diagnostic labels (benign vs. malignant/melanoma). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, weighted F1 score, and Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC). The ResNet+ViT hybrid achieved the highest accuracy of 85.1% and AUC of 0.932.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermatoscopic images obtained from Kaggle, sourced from the ISIC Archive which contains over 23,000 dermoscopic images collected from clinical centers worldwide. The study utilized a subset of 3,267 images.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning models with data augmentation (flipping, rotation, color jittering).
use.validation: Used for validation to assess model performance and generalization without data augmentation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive statistics (accuracy, AUC, etc.) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset (Validation Set).notes: The ResNet+ViT hybrid achieved the highest classification performance (85.1% accuracy, 0.932 AUC). MobileNetV2 offered the best computational efficiency (lowest parameters, smallest size, fastest inference). Hybrid models incurred significantly higher computational costs (inference times 3-4 times slower than MobileNetV2). The paper notes a discrepancy in the abstract/conclusion where ResNet+ViT accuracy is cited as 85.2% in one instance, but Table 2 lists it as 85.1%; the table value is used here.

architecture_used.main_model: Eight models across four categories: Traditional CNNs (AlexNet), Residual Networks (ResNet50, MobileNetV2, EfficientNet-B0), Vision Transformers (ViT-B/16), and Hybrid CNN-Transformer models (AlexNet+ViT, EfficientNet+ViT, ResNet+ViT)
architecture_used.pretraining: All models utilized weights pre-trained on ImageNet. Feature extraction layers were frozen for all models; only classification heads were adapted/trained.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels for all models except AlexNet, which used 227x227 pixels.

architecture_details.AlexNet: Traditional CNN with 5 convolutional layers followed by 3 fully connected layers. Feature extraction layers frozen.
architecture_details.ResNet50: 50-layer deep residual network with skip connections. Pre-trained backbone frozen, final FC layer replaced.
architecture_details.MobileNetV2: Lightweight architecture using depthwise separable convolutions and inverted residuals. Feature extraction layers frozen.
architecture_details.EfficientNet-B0: Scalable architecture using compound scaling to balance depth, width, and resolution. Pre-trained feature extraction layers frozen.
architecture_details.Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16): Adapted from NLP, treats image patches as tokens. Pre-trained backbone frozen, classification head replaced.
architecture_details.AlexNet+ViT Hybrid: Concatenates AlexNet features (dimension 4096) and ViT features (dimension 768) passed through a custom classifier.
architecture_details.EfficientNet+ViT Hybrid: Concatenates EfficientNet features (dimension 1280) and ViT features (dimension 768) processed through a multi-layer classifier.
architecture_details.ResNet+ViT Hybrid: Combines ResNet50 features (dimension 2048) and ViT features (dimension 768) for classification.

training_details.epochs: Maximum 50, with Early Stopping patience of 8 epochs
training_details.data_augmentation: Random horizontal flipping (p=0.5), random vertical flipping (p=0.5), random rotation (±15 degrees), color jittering (brightness 0.1, contrast 0.1, saturation 0.1, hue 0.05)
training_details.hardware: NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU, 64GB DDR5 RAM, AMD Ryzen 7800X3D CPU

architecture_used.classification_or_output_head: Custom classifier heads tailored for binary skin lesion classification (benign vs. malignant). For hybrids, features from CNN and ViT branches were concatenated before passing through the classifier.

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 224x224 pixels (227x227 for AlexNet)

Data augmentation during training: random horizontal flipping (p=0.5), random vertical flipping (p=0.5), random rotation (±15 degrees), and color jittering (brightness 0.1, contrast 0.1, saturation 0.1, hue 0.05)
Normalization using ImageNet mean (0.485, 0.456, 0.406) and standard deviation (0.229, 0.224, 0.225)
Validation set processed with normalization only, without data augmentation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with a probability of 0.5 during the training phase.
Applied with a probability of 0.5 during the training phase.
Applied within a range of ±15 degrees during the training phase.
Applied with parameters: brightness 0.1, contrast 0.1, saturation 0.1, and hue 0.05 during the training phase.

conclusions: Hybrid CNN-Transformer architectures consistently outperformed individual model types (traditional CNNs, residual networks, and vision transformers) in automated skin lesion classification.
The ResNet+ViT hybrid architecture achieved the highest classification performance with an accuracy of 85.1% and an AUC of 0.932.

MobileNetV2 offered the best computational efficiency, maintaining a reasonable accuracy of 79.1% while requiring the lowest parameters (4.0M), smallest model size (15.7 MB), and fastest inference time (1.97 ms), making it suitable for resource-constrained environments.
The performance improvements of hybrid models came with significant computational costs, including higher inference times and memory usage compared to efficient models like MobileNetV2.

Combining complementary feature extraction mechanisms from CNNs and transformers creates more robust representations for skin lesion classification, approaching the diagnostic accuracy of dermatologists reported in the literature.
The study highlights important trade-offs between classification performance and computational resources that must be considered for the practical deployment of automated diagnostic support tools.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; results are limited to a single subset of the ISIC dataset, and the authors explicitly state that further validation on external datasets and prospective clinical studies are necessary before real-world deployment.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons rely solely on descriptive statistics, limiting the robustness of claims regarding model superiority.

The dataset size is small (3,267 images total), which may limit the generalizability of the findings and the ability of data-hungry models like Vision Transformers to fully learn patterns without extensive augmentation.
There is a discrepancy in the reported accuracy for the ResNet+ViT hybrid model: the abstract and conclusion cite 85.2%, while Table 2 and the results section report 85.1%.

The models were evaluated on a balanced subset of the ISIC dataset (approx. 50/50 benign/malignant split), which does not reflect the class imbalance present in realistic clinical skin lesion datasets.

All models used transfer learning with frozen feature extraction layers and ImageNet pre-training, which may bias the results towards architectures that align well with natural image features rather than dermatoscopic-specific features.

The study highlights a significant performance-efficiency trade-off: hybrid models achieved higher accuracy but incurred 3-4 times slower inference times and significantly higher computational costs compared to efficient models like MobileNetV2.
The claimed 'dermatologist-level' accuracy is based on literature citations (65-80% or 75-84%) rather than direct comparison with human experts in this specific study.
